short.headline: Indian life expectancy rises to 72 years, but recent dip shows pandemic impact
short.dek: Life expectancy at birth in India has more than doubled since 1960, but the WHO reports a drop from 70.7 years in 2019 to 67.3 in 2021, reflecting COVID-19 disruptions.
short.body: Life expectancy at birth in India has risen from 45.6 years in 1960 to 72.2 years in 2024, according to the World Bank. However, the World Health Organization reports a decline from 70.7 years in 2019 to 67.3 years in 2021, a drop of 3.4 years that coincided with the COVID-19 pandemic. Women live longer than men: in 2021, female life expectancy was 69.0 years versus 65.8 for males. These data come with uncertainty ranges — for 2021, the overall estimate lies between 66.9 and 67.8 years. The long-term trend is upward, but the recent dip is a reminder that progress can be fragile.

article.title: How Long Do Indians Live? A Look at Life Expectancy Trends
article.standfirst: Life expectancy at birth in India has nearly doubled over six decades, but the pandemic years brought a notable dip. Here’s what the data from the WHO and World Bank show — and what they don’t.
article.bodyMarkdown:
Life expectancy at birth is a summary measure of mortality: it tells us the average number of years a newborn would live if current death rates stayed the same throughout their life. It is not a forecast, but a snapshot of a given year’s health conditions. For India, multiple sources track this number, and the picture they paint is one of steady long-term improvement interrupted by a sharp recent setback.

## The long arc of improvement

The World Bank’s data stretches back to 1960, when life expectancy at birth in India was 45.61 years. By 2024, that figure had risen to 72.235 years — an increase of nearly 27 years over six decades. This is a remarkable gain, driven by reductions in infant and child mortality, better nutrition, control of infectious diseases, and improvements in healthcare access.

The World Health Organization (WHO) provides a more granular picture more recently. In 2019, before the pandemic, life expectancy for both sexes combined was 70.7 years (with a 95% uncertainty interval of 70.3 to 71.3 years). Women lived longer than men: 72.4 years (72.0–72.9) for females, 69.2 years (68.7–69.8) for males.

## The COVID-19 dip

Then came the pandemic. In 2020, overall life expectancy fell slightly to 70.2 years (69.7–70.6). The decline was modest, but the following year saw a sharper drop. In 2021, the WHO estimate for India fell to 67.3 years (66.9–67.8) — a loss of 3.4 years from 2019. The 2021 figure is the lowest recorded in the WHO series since at least 2018 (which stood at 70.4 years).

The pandemic affected men and women differently. In 2021, female life expectancy was 69.0 years (68.6–69.5), while male life expectancy was 65.8 years (65.3–66.4). The gap between the sexes widened: in 2019, women outlived men by 3.2 years; in 2021, the gap was 3.2 years as well, but both numbers were lower. The male estimate for 2021 (65.8 years) is notably below the pre-pandemic level of 69.2 years in 2019, a drop of 3.4 years. For women, the decline from 72.4 to 69.0 was 3.4 years as well.

the caveat is that these are period measures: they reflect mortality conditions in a single year, not the actual lifespan of people born in that year. So the 2021 dip does not mean that a baby born in 2021 will die at 67; it means that if the death rates of 2021 persisted, that would be the average. As conditions improve, the actual cohort born in 2021 may live longer.

## Sex differences

Across all years, women in India have a higher life expectancy than men. This is consistent with global patterns. In the WHO data for 2020, female life expectancy was 72.2 years (71.7–72.7) vs. 68.4 for men (67.9–68.9). In 2021, the female advantage persisted. The reasons typically include biological factors (lower female mortality at all ages) and behavioural differences, but the data alone do not explain the causes.

## Data sources and differences

Two main sources are used here: the World Bank’s World Development Indicators (indicator SP.DYN.LE00.IN) and the WHO’s Global Health Observatory (indicator WHOSIS_000001). The World Bank reports a single figure for each year, derived from national statistics and UN estimates. The WHO provides estimates with uncertainty intervals and breakdowns by sex.

For 2021, the WHO’s combined-sex estimate is 67.3 years, while the World Bank’s latest figure is 72.235 years for 2024. These numbers are not directly comparable because they refer to different years and use different methodologies. The World Bank’s 2024 figure suggests a recovery after the pandemic dip, consistent with the notion that the 2021 drop was temporary.

The WHO data show a clear pandemic effect: from 2019 (70.7) to 2020 (70.2) to 2021 (67.3), a cumulative loss of 3.4 years. The exact impact of underreporting of deaths during the pandemic is unknown, but the downward trend is stark.

## What the data do not tell us

These national averages mask enormous variation. Life expectancy differs across states, between urban and rural areas, and by socioeconomic status. But the evidence packet contains no subnational data — the figures here are only for India as a whole. Readers should not assume that the national figure applies to any particular region or community.

Also, life expectancy is a period measure, not a cohort projection. It does not account for future improvements in healthcare or living conditions. It is a useful snapshot, not a prediction.

## Why it matters

Life expectancy is one of the most widely used indicators of population health. It encapsulates the combined effects of mortality rates at all ages. A rising life expectancy generally signals better health outcomes, while a decline — especially a sharp one — indicates a serious health crisis. The 3.4-year drop between 2019 and 2021 is among the largest recorded in modern Indian history, and it underscores the severe impact of the COVID-19 pandemic.

The recovery seen in the 2024 World Bank estimate (72.2 years) is encouraging, but it also highlights the fragility of health gains. Maintaining and improving life expectancy will require sustained investments in healthcare, disease prevention, and public health infrastructure.

## A note on uncertainty

Both sources provide uncertainty intervals. The WHO’s 95% confidence intervals for 2021 range from 66.9 to 67.8 years for the overall estimate. This means that the true value, given the data and model, is likely to fall within that range. The intervals reflect the limitations of data sources, statistical modelling, and sampling. They remind us that these numbers are estimates, not precise measurements.

## Conclusion

Indians today can expect to live about 72 years, a huge leap from the 46 years of the early 1960s. But the pandemic interrupted decades of progress, with life expectancy falling by about 3.4 years between 2019 and 2021. Women continue to outlive men by a few years. While the long-term trend is upward, the recent dip shows that health gains are not irreversible. These figures come from two respected international sources, each with its own methodology, and they should be interpreted with an understanding of what they measure — and what they do not.

evidence.question: How long do Indians live?
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.

forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
